1: nĕʾum-peša lārāšāʿ bĕqereb libbî (nĕʾum-p̄ešaʿ lārāšāʿ bĕqereb libbî): 'Transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart.' The noun nĕʾum ('oracle, utterance') — normally reserved for prophetic speech (nĕʾum YHWH, 'oracle of the LORD') — is here attributed to peša ('transgression'): sin functions as a false prophet within the wicked person, uttering its own counterfeit oracle. The phrase bĕqereb libbî ('within my heart') is textually difficult — some read libbô ('his heart'): the oracle of transgression lodges in the wicked man's inner life. The result: ʾên paḥad ʾĕlōhîm lĕneged ʿênāyw, 'there is no fear of God before his eyes' (Paul quotes this in Rom 3:18, completing his OT catena of universal sinfulness).
2: kî-heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw bĕʿênāyw limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ (kî-heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw bĕʿênāyw limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ): 'For he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated.' The hiphil heḥĕlîq ('to make smooth, flatter') applied reflexively — heḥĕlîq ʾēlāyw ('he flatters himself') — describes the self-deceptive sycophancy of the sinner's internal voice: the false oracle of v.1 tells him he is fine. The phrase limetsor ʾawōnô lišnōʾ, 'to find out his iniquity and hate it,' describes the moral investigation that would expose him — but self-flattery prevents this self-knowledge. The wicked man's sin is invisible to himself; the blindness is the sin's deepest effect.
3: dibrê pîw ʾāwen ûmirmâ ḥādal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîb (diḇrê p̄îw ʾāwen ûmirmâ ḥāḏal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîḇ): 'The words of his mouth are trouble and deceit; he has ceased to act wisely and do good.' The mouth (peh) is again the diagnostic organ: dibrê ʾāwen ûmirmâ ('words of wickedness and deceit') reveal the heart's corruption (cf. Matt 12:34). The phrase ḥādal lĕhaśkîl lĕhêṭîb, 'he has ceased to act wisely and do good,' uses the perfect ḥādal ('to cease, stop') — the cessation of wisdom and goodness is not a gradual decline but an accomplished break. The wicked man has definitively stopped the two activities that define the righteous: śākal (act wisely) and hêṭîb (do good/act beneficially).
4: ʾāwen yĕḥaššōb ʿal-miškābô yityaṣṣēb ʿal-derek lōʾ-ṭôb rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās (ʾāwen yĕḥaššōḇ ʿal-mišḵāḇô yiṯyaṣṣēḇ ʿal-ḏereḵ lōʾ-ṭôḇ rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās): 'He plots trouble on his bed; he sets himself in a way that is not good; he does not reject evil.' The bedroom (miškāb) is where unguarded thought happens — and the wicked man's nocturnal activity is planning ʾāwen ('iniquity, wickedness'). The hitpael yityaṣṣēb ('he stations himself, sets himself') describes deliberate positioning on derek lōʾ-ṭôb ('a way that is not good') — understated irony: it is not merely 'not good' but actively evil. The conclusion rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās, 'evil he does not reject,' completes the portrait: night-scheming, deliberate path-taking, and the refusal to refuse evil. This is the full portrait of the hardened wicked, in contrast to the entering worshippers of Ps 24.
5: YHWH baššāmayim ḥasdekā ʾĕmûnātĕkā ʿad-šĕḥāqîm (YHWH baššāmayim ḥasdĕḵā ʾĕmûnāṯĕḵā ʿaḏ-šĕḥāqîm): 'Your steadfast love, O LORD, extends to the heavens; your faithfulness to the clouds.' After the portrait of the wicked (vv.1-4), the psalm pivots sharply to the fullness of divine character. The spatial imagery — baššāmayim ('to the heavens') and ʿad-šĕḥāqîm ('to the clouds') — uses the vertical dimension to express limitlessness: ḥesed ('steadfast love') and ʾĕmûnâ ('faithfulness, reliability') are not bounded attributes but reach upward without ceiling. The contrast is complete: the wicked's reach is to his bed and to wickedness; YHWH's reach is to the sky.
6: ṣidqātĕkā kĕhārê ʾēl mišpāṭêkā tĕhôm rabbâ (ṣiḏqāṯĕḵā kĕhārê ʾēl mišpāṭêkā ṯĕhôm rabbâ): 'Your righteousness is like the mountains of God; your judgments are like the great deep.' The two similes create a vertical pair: ṣĕdāqâ ('righteousness') compared to hārê ʾēl ('mountains of God,' i.e., mighty/towering mountains) — high and immovable. mišpāṭîm ('judgments') compared to tĕhôm rabbâ ('the great deep') — the primordial abyss whose depth is unfathomable. Both are inaccessible to full human comprehension but utterly reliable: the righteousness and judgment of YHWH are as real and as beyond human measure as the mountains and the ocean depths. Paul's doxology in Rom 11:33 echoes this: 'how unsearchable are his judgments!'
7: mah-yāqār ḥasdĕkā ʾĕlōhîm ûbĕnê ʾādām bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā yeḥĕsāyûn (mah-yāqār ḥasdĕḵā ʾĕlōhîm ûḇĕnê ʾāḏām bĕṣēl kĕnāp̄êḵā yeḥĕsāyûn): 'How precious is your steadfast love, O God! The children of man take refuge in the shadow of your wings.' The exclamatory mah-yāqār ('how precious!') from yāqar ('to be precious, costly') applies economic language of extreme value to ḥesed. The image of refuge bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā ('in the shadow of your wings') — the shadow being cool, protective, and intimate — draws on the mother-bird sheltering her young (Deut 32:11; Ps 17:8; 57:1; Matt 23:37). The phrase ûbĕnê ʾādām ('the children of man') universalizes the refuge: it is available to all humanity, not only to Israel, which is the psalm's implicit mission theology.
8: yirwĕyûn middešen bêtekā wĕnaḥal ʿădānêkā tašqēm (yirwĕyûn middešen bêṯeḵā wĕnaḥal ʿăḏānêḵā ṯašqēm): 'They feast on the abundance of your house, and you give them drink from the river of your delights.' The verb rāwâ ('to be saturated, drink one's fill') combined with middešen bêtekā ('from the richness/fat of your house') describes the sanctuary meal — the ḥesed of YHWH materialized as rich food. The phrase naḥal ʿădānêkā ('the river of your delights') uses the noun ʿeden — a word connected to 'Eden' (Gen 2:8) and meaning 'luxury, delight.' The river of YHWH's delights is the antithesis of the desert and the image of eschatological abundance (cf. Rev 22:1-2, 'the river of the water of life').
9: kî ʿimmĕkā mĕqôr ḥayyîm bĕʾôrkā nirʾeh-ʾôr (kî ʿimmĕḵā mĕqôr ḥayyîm bĕʾôrĕḵā nirʾeh-ʾôr): 'For with you is the fountain of life; in your light do we see light.' The phrase mĕqôr ḥayyîm ('fountain of life') identifies YHWH as the source of all vitality — life does not merely come from YHWH but is located ʿimmĕkā ('with you'). The chiastic bĕʾôrkā nirʾeh-ʾôr ('in your light we see light') is among the most theologically dense phrases in the OT: the light by which we perceive anything is itself derived from divine light. John's prologue echoes this: 'In him was life, and the life was the light of men' (John 1:4). The fountain and the light image together describe YHWH as the ground of both existence (life) and intelligibility (light).
10: mĕšōk ḥasdĕkā lĕyōdĕʿêkā wĕṣidqātĕkā lĕyišrê-lēb (mĕšōk ḥasdĕḵā lĕyôḏĕʿêḵā wĕṣiḏqāṯĕḵā lĕyišrê-lēḇ): 'Continue your steadfast love to those who know you, and your righteousness to the upright of heart!' The imperative mĕšōk ('draw out, prolong') from māšak ('to draw, extend') — as in drawing a line — applied to ḥesed asks for its continuation: not a new gift but the extension of an established pattern. The recipients lĕyōdĕʿêkā ('those who know you') and lĕyišrê-lēb ('the upright of heart') define the covenant community by knowledge and integrity — both qualities opposite to the wicked of vv.1-4.
11: ʾal-tĕbôʾēnî regel gaʾăwâ wĕyad-rĕšāʿîm ʾal-tĕnidēnî (ʾal-ṯĕḇôʾēnî regel gaʾăwâ wĕyaḏ-rĕšāʿîm ʾal-ṯĕniḏēnî): 'Let not the foot of arrogance come upon me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away.' The spatial language of the wicked — regel gaʾăwâ ('the foot of pride/arrogance') and yad rĕšāʿîm ('the hand of the wicked') — frames the threat as a territorial incursion: arrogance presses down with its foot, wickedness reaches with its hand to displace. The noun gaʾăwâ ('pride') is the sin that fuels the wicked of vv.1-4: the oracle of transgression (v.1) produces the prideful self-flattery (v.2) that enables all subsequent wrongdoing.
12: šām nāplû pōʿălê ʾāwen dĕḥû wĕlōʾ-yākĕlû qûm (šām nāplû p̄ōʿălê ʾāwen dĕḥû wĕlōʾ-yāḵĕlû qûm): 'There the evildoers lie fallen; they are thrust down, unable to rise.' The deictic šām ('there!') points to a specific location of judgment — perhaps the place where the wicked fell in past divine action, serving as a sign. The verbs nāplû ('they have fallen'), dĕḥû ('they were thrust down'), and lōʾ-yāḵĕlû qûm ('they are unable to rise') describe the finality of the wicked's collapse: the fall is complete, the fall is passive (thrust down, not merely stumbled), and there is no recovery. Against the living waters of v.9 and the refuge under divine wings of v.7, the wicked have chosen the darkness of their bed-plotting (v.4) — and this is its end. The psalm closes with the scene of the wicked prostrate and immobile, the mirror-image of the righteous 'satisfied with your fat' (v.8).
